title: Primary tumor descriptor
summary: T is driven by the highest qualifying feature: size, local invasion, airway/pleural features, or separate tumor nodules.
bullets: Use the largest invasive dimension when assigning a size-based T category.
A qualifying invasion or separate-nodule rule can outrank size.
Contralateral pulmonary nodules are metastatic disease rather than a higher T category.
changedFrom8th: No T category change was adopted for the 9th edition; the learning task is applying the existing T rules consistently.

title: T1: 3 cm or smaller without higher-risk features
summary: T1 is subdivided by size: T1a is 1 cm or smaller, T1b is greater than 1 to 2 cm, and T1c is greater than 2 to 3 cm.
bullets: T1 tumors are surrounded by lung or visceral pleura, or are in a lobar or more peripheral bronchus.
A superficial spreading tumor limited to the bronchial wall remains T1 even if it extends proximally.
Any higher qualifying invasion or separate-nodule feature should override the size-only T1 call.
changedFrom8th: The 9th edition keeps the 8th-edition T1 cut points.

title: T2: 3-5 cm or selected airway/pleural features
summary: T2a covers tumors greater than 3 to 4 cm or selected T2 features; T2b covers tumors greater than 4 to 5 cm.
bullets: Visceral pleural invasion, adjacent-lobe invasion, main bronchus involvement without carina, and hilar fat invasion are T2a features unless size assigns a higher T.
Atelectasis or obstructive pneumonitis extending to the hilar region remains a T2 feature.
If atelectasis/pneumonitis is present with a measurable tumor greater than 4 to 5 cm, size drives T2b.
changedFrom8th: T2 wording remains materially unchanged in the 9th edition; teach atelectasis/pneumonitis as a T2 feature, not a new 9th-edition change.

title: T3: 5-7 cm, selected invasion, or same-lobe nodules
summary: T3 includes tumors greater than 5 to 7 cm, invasion of selected nearby structures, or separate tumor nodules in the same lobe.
bullets: Parietal pleura or chest wall invasion is T3.
Pericardium, phrenic nerve, azygos vein, thoracic nerve root, or stellate ganglion invasion is T3.
Separate tumor nodules in the same lobe as the primary are T3.
changedFrom8th: The 9th edition did not change the T3 category, although survival analyses re-tested the descriptors.

title: T4: greater than 7 cm, major invasion, or different ipsilateral lobe nodules
summary: T4 includes tumors greater than 7 cm, invasion of major mediastinal or neurovascular structures, or separate nodules in a different ipsilateral lobe.
bullets: Mediastinum, thymus, trachea, carina, recurrent laryngeal nerve, vagus nerve, esophagus, and diaphragm invasion are T4 features.
Heart, great vessel, subclavian vessel, vertebral body, spinal canal, cervical nerve root, and brachial plexus invasion are T4 features.
Separate tumor nodules in a different ipsilateral lobe are T4.
changedFrom8th: The 9th edition keeps the 8th-edition T4 descriptor set.

title: Regional nodal descriptor
summary: N is determined by laterality, nodal basin, and in the 9th edition the number of involved N2 stations.
bullets: Ipsilateral hilar, peribronchial, and intrapulmonary nodes are N1.
Ipsilateral mediastinal and subcarinal nodes are N2.
Contralateral mediastinal or hilar nodes, plus scalene/supraclavicular nodes, are N3.
changedFrom8th: The main N update is subdivision of N2 into N2a for one N2 station and N2b for multiple N2 stations.

title: N2a versus N2b
summary: N2a means a single ipsilateral mediastinal or subcarinal station is involved; N2b means more than one such station is involved.
bullets: Station 7 is a subcarinal N2 station regardless of primary tumor side.
For lateralized mediastinal stations such as 2R/2L and 4R/4L, primary tumor side determines N2 versus N3.
Counting stations matters because some TNM combinations move when N2a becomes N2b.
changedFrom8th: 8th edition used a single N2 category; 9th edition separates single-station and multi-station N2 disease.

title: N3: contralateral or supraclavicular/scalene disease
summary: N3 outranks N1 and N2 whenever a positive node is contralateral or in scalene/supraclavicular territory.
bullets: Contralateral station 2 or 4 is N3.
Contralateral hilar or intrapulmonary disease is also N3.
Scalene or supraclavicular nodal involvement is N3 on either side.
changedFrom8th: The N3 definition is carried forward; the 9th-edition novelty is the additional detail below N2.

title: Distant metastasis descriptor
summary: M describes pleural/pericardial spread, contralateral pulmonary nodules, and extrathoracic metastasis burden.
bullets: M1a includes malignant pleural or pericardial effusion, pleural/pericardial nodules, or a contralateral lung nodule.
M1b is a single extrathoracic metastasis in one organ system.
M1c is reserved for multiple extrathoracic metastases and is now subdivided.
changedFrom8th: The 9th edition divides M1c into M1c1 and M1c2.

title: M1c1 versus M1c2
summary: M1c1 means multiple extrathoracic metastases in one organ system; M1c2 means multiple extrathoracic metastases in more than one organ system.
bullets: Multiple bone lesions remain one organ system and are M1c1.
Multiple liver lesions remain one organ system and are M1c1.
Bone plus liver disease crosses organ systems and is M1c2.
changedFrom8th: 8th edition grouped these as M1c; 9th edition separates single-organ-system from multi-organ-system metastatic burden.

title: Selected 9th-edition stage-group moves
summary: Stage groups were adjusted to reflect N2a/N2b and contemporary prognosis while preserving treatment judgment.
bullets: T1N1 is stage IIA.
T1N2a is stage IIB.
T2N2b is stage IIIB.
T3N2a is stage IIIA.
changedFrom8th: These are the most visible stage-group shifts for learners using the 9th-edition matrix.
